---
title: "Opening Ceremony Austie"
---

to do

-   ~~read the JOhn egan report~~

-   adapt portlaw ceremony and weave it in with dungarvan

-   ~~john egan aftermath~~

-   call ?Tommy Mooney about the Waterford Brigades

------------------------------------------------------------------------

-    **and, given the circumstances, that was the hard part done, as we shall see.**

It was a Monday morning and Austie was due to greet Lemass whenever he got down from Dublin in the car. An ad had gone out in the previous week's Observer, informing people of the official opening ceremony and urging them to decorate the town in anticipation of the big, and they responded by decking out the main streets with bunting and banners of all colours, shapes and sizes.

Lemass was a busy man and that contributed to the lengthy delay to this celebration. He had been opening factories for literally years at this stage, as new factories had been popping up all over the country since the Fianna Fail government, in which he was Minister for Industry and Commerce, had begun a policy of self-sufficiency in 1932, getting Ireland to manufacture as much of its needs as possible. Indeed he had been opening industrial facilities for so long that one cartoonist showed him in a wheelchair, with a key in his hand, still opening factories. Thus the Dungarvan tannery had been operation for fully eighteen months and they had been selling their leather for twelve months. Austie was glad of the delay because it meant they could show off the factory in full flow, free of the teething errors that you would expect from such a virgin operation.

**Portlaw Opening**

Austie and his colleagues had a good idea of how the day would pan out because many of them had already been to the opening ceremony at Irish Tanners in Portlaw in September 1935, a little before Dickens Leather opened its doors in October 1935. That day, Lemass had come from Dublin and was greeted near the factory in the centre of curious, hand-shaped village. There was a pipe band and a guard of honour and speeches afterwards, followed by a big lunch, although the sheer tininess of Portlaw was highlighted by the fact that there was no hotel there to cook the food on site, so they had to convey the lunch from Waterford.

This would not be the case for a town as large and well-serviced as Dungarvan. A big difference between the sites was that Portlaw mostly existed in order to house workers for the factory. In fact the village had been constructed to house the workers of a nineteenth-century cotton factory which had long since gone under. Reviving the site for a tannery was a chance for the state to symbolically revive its manufacturing past. By contrast, Dungarvan was a good-sized town of almost five thousand people in dire need of a big employer for its people. Dungarvan was also well-used to hosting people as its population would swell in the summer, either with those staying in the town or to feed those passing through on their way west.

By the time Lemass arrived in Abbeyside, across the Causeway bridge, a

Austie sauntered down to the Causeway Bridge to meet Lemass on arrival. He came with fellow directors Joe Molony and Reginald Dickens himself, but also some members of the counci

**Vincent Crowley**

The Chairman of Irish Tanners was an accountant from Dublin called Vincent Crowley, who would also be auditor for Dickens Leather. Crowley was a man on a mission who had his finger in a huge number of financial pies. In 1925, he became auditor of the Knights of Columbanus, a secretive and high-powered order of lay Catholics, whose main goal was to help replace the old Protestant elites in Dublin with Catholics. And with the enormous number of new state-sponsored industries, this frequently involved getting the state to avail of the services of Crowley's company, Kennedy, Crowley and Co. Along the way, Crowley became close friends with Lemass, and the two functioned in tandem as disruptors to the status quo. Lemass by shaking up the department staff and getting it to function properly

-   along the way he became close to Lemass and very much had his ear

Weather differences

he had been a peripheral attendee at the opening ceremony at Irish Tenners in Portlaw in 1935, where of course Ken was also one of the main men and Lemass was also there to open it. Vincent Crowley was the manager of the

he had been opening factories for literally years at this point so this was really just another day in the office for him. But for the people of Dungarvan, today was an enormous day, bigger than St Patrick's Day in some ways, and like nothing the town had ever seen. An ad had gone out the week before

r Austie too it was huge because it marked the end of the beginning of the factory. and they had been selling leather **for a good twelve months now**.

The official opening ceremony of Dickens Leather Factory was fully eighteen months after the tannery opened its doors so operations were in full swing by the time the town went into full celebration mode. An advertisement went out the week before requesting people to erect colourful bunting and banners

The whole town had come out to cheer them on.

how proud and enthusiastic that he and the whole town had been at that point, how the future looked so bright for all, how it was so bright back then**, before the war slowed down all the progress they had made.**

It was like St Patrick's Day only bigger and louder. Minister Sean Lemass had come down for the jamboree and he was met by Austie and the other directors on the Abbeyside side of the Causeway.

The whole town came out to cheer us on that day. It was like St Patrick's day only bigger and louder. I remember Lemass came down in his car and we met him over the Causeway bridge

-   *there would be a great turn-out of people at public meetings which you would not get at all now, but of course there was no other mans of commmucication except public meetings and no television serven and the radio was very inadequate, if used at all for political purposese*

-   Lemass

Minister Sean Lemass had come down for the jamboree, just as he had in Portlaw in '35 when Austie was an onlooker for the day. It was good to see the set up that day and to compare it to here. You had Lemass there at both, and the Bishop and the parish priest, the directors and **get the list of attendees at portlaw opening versus dickens**

But Portlaw was hardly a town. They had to drive in the food from Waterford because there was no hotel in the town to cook the dinner for the dozens of people

Dungarvan was a great place to be and the factory was exactly what it needed. A proper town with jobs for all. Up to now, you either had a family business in the town or you had work on the farms nearby. There was very little for the young fellas walking round or standing on street corners with nothing to do.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

The factory had actually been in production for eighteen months by the time the official opening ceremony was due to take place, but the big day had finally come. Austie lay in the bath, puffing away, and meditating on the enormity of the day, of the situation, of the life he had made for himself and the livelihoods he had created. How many children would go to bed that night with a full belly on account of the factory? There must be several hundreds. Ken always said that. Employ family men because then you feed more mouths and do more good.

The hardest work was already behind them and it was good to be celebrating working machines. Anyone can say what they want over a new machine but it's another thing entirely to get the machine turning, and the lads trained, and the leather leaving the place in a condition that would satisfy a boot-maker. The factory had proved to be a huge benefit not just to the town, but to the country, even in that short space of time, because shoes made in Ireland could now use Irish leather, and it looked like that would only increase in the years to come.

There was politics in the air too, the sort you could taste. Lemass had been made Minister for Industry and Commerce again after the July election, and Fianna Fáil had its hand on the wheel. Lemass had been opening factories for years at that stage, so long that people joked you’d see him some day being wheeled up to a gate with a pair of scissors still in his hand, ready to cut the ribbon. For him, Dungarvan was just another opening. For Dungarvan, it was the day the town was given permission to feel proud.

An advertisement had gone out beforehand, requesting the public to turn the town out in flags and bunting, and they did. Varicoloured streamers on the main streets, banners welcoming the Minister, the whole place dressed up like a communion morning, only louder. The Gardaí were out in force too, not because Dungarvan was a wild town, but because a Minister in the Free State drew a certain kind of attention, and they liked to make a show of order when they were being watched.

Lemass alighted in Abbeyside, across the Causeway Bridge, and we met him there. Father T. B. Walsh was the first man you’d see at an occasion like that, because he was Chairman of the Dungarvan Industrial Development Association and co-founder of the local factories in everything but name. Joe Molony was there too, solid and practical, a man with businesses on the quay and in the town, who understood what it meant to have money moving through local hands. And there was me, Dr. Michael Augustine Casey, a director of Dickens Leather, and the local doctor to half the people cheering on the street.

Then there was Kennedy O’Brien, Chairman, a leather merchant out of Dublin, and the nearest thing Ireland had to a leather baron in those days. And there was Reginald Dickens, Managing Director, Northampton man, technical man, the English partner with the know-how and the hunger. The newspapers would later talk about it like it was all local enterprise, and it was, for the men who worked in the place, but the directors were a mix of locals and bigger fish and that was the reality of it.

Once the men of influence were assembled, the procession began to slither through the decorated streets to the roars of the crowd, cheer after cheer rent from the air like a gale. The Waterford Brass and Reed Band led the parade playing familiar airs with a nationalistic bent. The crowd followed behind like mass-goers filing up for communion, only here the host was a promise: steady work.

On Quay Street they were waiting for us — the factory men, a hundred and more of them, and you could see the way they stood: proud but wary, because they knew as well as I did that factories are not miracles. They are machines, and machines demand.

A temporary stand had been erected. Lemass was greeted by Ken O’Brien in his capacity as Chairman. The band played the National Anthem while the crowd looked on deep as tide. Then the speeches began. The Urban District Council. Father Walsh. Two Fianna Fáil cumainn, the Pádraig Pearse and the Cathal Brugha. And then someone from the workforce spoke too — unnamed in the reports, of course, as if the worker was a type of man rather than a person with a family and a sore back.[\[2\]](https://www.notion.so/Opening-Ceremony-2c04cfb3616480dea117eb920d7efe9b?pvs=21)

Lemass made the last public address. He thanked and congratulated the directors and the workers and his political colleagues, and he spoke to the crowd about employment and prosperity, about what this would do for the town. And sure, the figures themselves were speaking. In those early days there were 120 operators employed, mostly local men, and the weekly payroll was about £300 — a great financial boost to Dungarvan, as the report put it, and that was not exaggeration. You could feel it in the town already: cash in pockets, bills being paid, a bit of confidence where there had only been talk.[\[1\]](https://www.notion.so/Opening-Ceremony-2b04cfb36164805a9ed0dfbf70b9604f?pvs=21)

Afterwards Ken presented Lemass with the symbolic gold key to the factory. Then the church took its turn, because in Ireland you do not open a factory without blessing it. *Faith of Our Fathers* was played, and His Lordship Dr. Jeremiah Kinane, Bishop of Waterford and Lismore, blessed the place solemnly, assisted by the parish priest and by Father Walsh. There was something almost comical about it — men blessing modern machinery with old words — but I never mocked it. In a town like ours, the blessing was another kind of permission: it meant the factory was not a foreign body. It belonged.

Dickens then brought the Bishop and the Minister on a tour of inspection. Reggie was at his best when he could show machines and process, the logic of leather made visible. The place had been corn and coal stores once, and now it was something else entirely, altered and adapted for the purpose, and the reports made a point of that too — the extensive alterations, the way the company later acquired the adjoining premises running into Church Street, Barry’s Corn Stores.[\[1\]](https://www.notion.so/Opening-Ceremony-2b04cfb36164805a9ed0dfbf70b9604f?pvs=21)

The public were let in afterwards. The local hordes poured through, and you did wonder how well supervised the machines were with children running around, fingers where fingers shouldn’t be. But that was the town claiming it. People wanted to see where the money was made, where the smell came from, where their sons were spending their days.

And somewhere in the middle of all the bunting and bands and speeches, I held on to the details that mattered more than the ceremony: that the share issue had been oversubscribed and closed within minutes; that there was real confidence, real appetite, real backing; that Dickens Leather had obtained what the report called a “virtual monopoly” of the Free State market for its products; and that we were the only factory in the Free State producing certain lining and upper leathers for the boot and shoe trade, and other lines besides — gloving, clothing, belt and braces, fancy trades.[\[1\]](https://www.notion.so/Opening-Ceremony-2b04cfb36164805a9ed0dfbf70b9604f?pvs=21)[\[2\]](https://www.notion.so/Opening-Ceremony-2c04cfb3616480dea117eb920d7efe9b?pvs=21)

The papers loved those phrases. So did Lemass. The town loved the parade.

But later, in the bath, I found myself thinking instead of the training classes. The lads enrolled in special classes at the old Technical School, learning how to handle the machines, because none of us had been born to this. That was the true opening ceremony, in my view — not the gold key, but the first young fella who learned to run a machine without fear and brought home a wage packet that didn’t depend on the sea, or the seasons, or a cousin in England.[\[1\]](https://www.notion.so/Opening-Ceremony-2b04cfb36164805a9ed0dfbf70b9604f?pvs=21)
